data.title: Value investors still holding onto Microsoft, ServiceNow despite AI concerns: BNP
data.description:
Despite concerns about artificial intelligence disrupting the enterprise software space, value investors are still holding onto their stakes in Microsoft (MSFT) and ServiceNow (NOW),  at least for now, investment firm BNP Paribas said. 

“Seemingly daily negative newsflow and sector underperformance has worn down long investors, while dip buyers have been repeatedly penalized,” analyst Stefan Slowinski wrote in a note to clients. “Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) success with Claude Code, along with excitement around Claude Cowork, and now uncertainty created by OpenClaw, have caused most investors to label the sector as ‘too hard’ for now.”

Despite that, Slowinski said most of the conversations with investors lately have centered around whether they should hold onto their software positions. “The two names most mentioned where investors are still holding positions were Microsoft (unsurprisingly) and ServiceNow,” Slowinski added. “Accenture (ACN) was also mentioned.”

Slowinski conceded that investor concerns are real and not to be ignored, as Anthropic and OpenAI's (OPENAI) respective successes with Claude Code and Codex create headwinds.

However, investors also wonder that even if the incumbents can thwart off the upstarts, there may be some “pricing pressure” from the start-ups, along with declines in margins amid increased R&D and model spending. “Many investors question the future ‘value add’ of software vendors with many investors repeating the ‘SaaS vendors simply become resellers of tokens’ concern,” Slowinski added.

And while there may be a lot of negativity surrounding the space, many investors believe the big tech incumbents are “best positioned to eventually win,” pointing to their success in the cloud.

Specifically for Microsoft, the fear is that the company may not take advantage of its so-called “right to win” in the enterprise.

“Microsoft’s strategic approach toward model development and infrastructure build continues to shift,” Slowinski added. “While many argue the valuation is now too cheap, especially relative to hyperscaler peers considering Microsoft’s superior FCF, investors struggle to identify a catalyst with the company seemingly taking Azure beats off the table.”

And for ServiceNow, investors are concerned about the “mixed feedback” on its AI products, as well as the cautious guidance for the first quarter.

data.title: Sphere Entertainment, Orange come out on top in communication services quant picks ahead of Q1 earnings
data.description:
The S&P 500’s Communication Services (XLC) saw a challenging first quarter as geopolitical tension in the Middle East and economic uncertainty mostly dictated investor sentiment.

The sector, which holds 9% weightage on the S&P 500 and boasts of companies including Google-parent Alphabet (GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META), declined 5.17% during the first quarter. This is in comparison to the 4.8% fall in the broader S&P 500 Index.

While the energy sector was a winner during the quarter as a spike in crude prices following disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz led to gains in oil and gas companies, other sectors, including financials (XLF) and technology (XLK) saw sharp sell-offs. Major U.S. indexes also experienced a notable downturn during the quarter, with losses coming mostly in late February after the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran.

According to Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system, the Communication Services sector has an average health score of 3.22 out of 5, based on 100 stocks with market capitalizations above $2 billion.

Looking into individual companies in the sector, 17 stocks are rated as Buy and above based on Quant Ratings, 77 stocks are rated Neutral, and six are considered Sell or lower, with ratings based on valuation, growth, profitability, momentum, and EPS revision.

Here are the top and bottom-rated 5 quant stocks ahead of the upcoming earnings season:

The pre-earnings quantitative breakdown shows that top-rated stocks are being driven primarily by profitability and momentum, while the low-rated names reflect a sharp decline in the valuation factor.

Top 5 stocks: Sphere Entertainment (SPHR), Quant Rating 4.97, Strong Buy

Orange (ORANY), Quant Rating 4.89, Strong Buy

Turkcell Iletisim Hizmetleri (TKC), Quant Rating 4.86, Strong Buy

TIM (TIMB), Quant Rating 4.80, Strong Buy

Millicom International Cellular (TIGO), Quant Rating 4.73, Strong Buy

Bottom 5 stocks: Tencent Music Entertainment Group (TME), Quant Rating 1.43, Strong Sell

Grindr (GRND), Quant Rating 1.72, Sell

Baidu (BAIDF), Quant Rating 1.90, Sell

Trade Desk (TTD), Quant Rating 1.96, Sell

Formula One Group (FWONA), Quant Rating 1.98, Sell

SA Analyst has a Buy rating on S&P 500’s Communication Services (XLC) with a score of 3.00.

data.title: Walmart shares snap six-session winning streak
data.description:
Walmart (WMT) shares snapped six straight sessions of gains on Tuesday, as the stock was 3% lower at $122.95.

The global retail and wholesale giant gained over 3% in the preceding six sessions. So far this year, the stock has added nearly 11%, compared to the 4% drop in the broader S&P 500 Index (SP500).

WMT is down 1% over the past one month.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, WMT has a Hold rating with a score of 3.4 out of 5. The company received A+ in the prospect of profitability, while it received C in the growth factor.

Seeking Alpha analysts are pessimistic and see the stock as a Sell.

Turning to the Wall Street, 39 analysts have given the stock a Buy or above rating. Three gave the stock hold recommendation, while one gave Strong Sell rating to WMT.

 
 
 

“Competitive threats from Amazon, inflation, and rising oil prices may constrain WMT's pricing flexibility and profitability,” pointed out a recent Seeking Alpha analysis.

data.title: Why this oil rally isn’t crashing stocks—Deutsche Bank breaks it down
data.description:
Despite a sharp rise in oil prices, global equity markets have shown notable resilience, prompting analysts at Deutsche Bank to argue that the current selloff differs meaningfully from past energy-driven downturns.

Brent crude has remained above $100 per barrel for nearly a month, yet major benchmarks such as the S&P 500 (SP500) and Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) are only modestly below their record highs, declining roughly 5–6%. Historically, comparable oil shocks have triggered far steeper equity losses. 

The investment bank attributes this divergence to three key factors. First, futures markets suggest investors expect the current geopolitical tensions to be short-lived. While spot Brent prices hover around $111 per barrel, contracts further out indicate a significant decline, with six-month and twelve-month prices falling into the low $80s and upper $70s, respectively. 

Second, incoming economic data continues to signal expansion rather than contraction, easing fears of an oil-induced recession. 

Finally, central banks have so far refrained from implementing aggressive tightening measures. This stands in contrast to prior episodes—such as the rapid rate hikes of 2022 and the inflation-fighting policies of Paul Volcker—which amplified market stress. 

Together, these factors are helping cushion risk assets despite elevated energy prices, Deutsche Bank expressed. 

Market Tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ). 

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), (AMLP), and (IXC).

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL). 

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).

data.title: Tractor Supply faces risk from subdued discretionary spending—BofA Securities
data.description:
BofA Securities reinstated coverage of Tractor Supply Company (TSCO) with a Neutral rating, as the company’s exposure to rising gas/diesel prices and subdued spending on big-ticket items will limit upside potential in the company’s profits, while downside risk on shares is partially mitigated by higher corn prices used in animal feed.

“We remain relatively cautious on near-term demand; we view the majority of TSCO’s business as either discretionary or highly competitive,” said analyst Christopher Nardone, noting a pullback in purchases of big-ticket categories last year due to a more cautious consumer.

“Tractor Supply could see pressure on big-ticket categories this spring given broader uncertainty and cost inflation, which could drive repair versus replacement activity,” Nardone writes.

While he expects to see progress on direct sales, final mile, and pet and animal medications, it will take some time for the summation of these initiatives to be a significant needle mover on a $15B sales base.

Nardone set a $47 price target for Tractor Supply (TSCO) representing 3% upside from Monday’s closing price.

data.title: Wall Street sinks as ceasefire hopes diminish; U.S. hits military targets on Kharg Island
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were in the red on Tuesday as reports surfaced that the U.S. has attacked military targets on Kharg Island, while President Donald Trump threatened Iran's “imminent destruction” if the country does not agree to a ceasefire.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.3%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.5%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.4%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points higher at 4.36%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 1 basis point to 3.87%.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were higher at $114, while Brent (CO1:COM) was at $109 per barrel.

“A whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again. I don’t want that to happen, but it probably will,” Trump said on Truth Social, ahead of the 8 p.m. deadline he set for the opening of the Strait of Hormuz.

“We will find out tonight, one of the most important moments in the long and complex history of the world. 47 years of extortion, corruption, and death will finally end,” he wrote.

Iran has allegedly responded by cutting off communication with the U.S.

In addition, the U.S. has reportedly attacked military targets on Kharg Island as the 8 p.m. ET deadline arrives. According to media reports, electricity supply was disrupted in parts of the Iranian island after projectiles struck transmission lines.

In economic news, durable goods orders fell more than expected in February.

Consumers' inflation expectations increased short- and medium-term, and held unchanged at the longer-term outlook, as gas price growth expectations jumped to the highest level since March 2022, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.

The consumer credit data is due during market hours.

data.title: Current oil shock will create unprecedented crisis due to high debt and deficit levels
data.description:
The current oil shock stemming from the Iran war is fundamentally different from past crises because the world has entered it with unprecedented levels of debt and deficits, Ruchir Sharma, chairman of Rockefeller International, warned in an interview with CNBC.

While the IEA has said this crisis is worse than those of 1973, 1979, and 2022 combined, Sharma argues the economic angle presents an even more troubling picture that will severely limit how governments can respond.

Sharma pointed to specific fiscal metrics that underscore the challenge facing developed economies. The average budget deficit among developed countries sits at nearly 4% of GDP, with the U.S. leading at close to 6%—a figure that could climb to 7% this year.

Government debt levels have also reached historic highs, exceeding 100% of GDP in America and other developed nations. These elevated debt levels mean governments lack the fiscal flexibility to shield their economies from the shock.

“Never before has the world entered any sort of a crisis with such high debt and deficit levels,” Sharma said, adding that “this is going to limit the ability of governments to cushion the economic impact.”

The bond market’s behavior during this crisis has been notably unusual. Rather than falling as investors seek safe havens, bond yields have risen—not because of inflation expectations, which remain relatively anchored, but because of mounting concerns about debt sustainability and the rising term premium.

Sharma identified specific warning signs that some fiscal “red lines” are being crossed. Most notably, the interest expense on U.S. debt now exceeds the entire defense budget, a metric that has historically signaled trouble for major global powers facing economic turmoil.

The implications grow more severe the longer the disruption continues.

“The longer this lasts, the more problematic that becomes,” Sharma warned, emphasizing that the combination of unprecedented debt burdens and limited government intervention capacity creates a uniquely challenging environment for global economic stability.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Consumers' short-term inflation expectations climb as gas price growth expectations spike
  • Durable goods orders fall more than expected in February
  • White House's Hassett says AI, fiscal policy should allow Fed to resume rate cuts
  • Markets are pricing in normalization from the Iran war; upcoming economic data may paint a clearer picture
  • Dollar eases as geopolitical uncertainty keeps markets cautious: Currency Recap

data.title: Palo Alto Networks continues to expand margins despite M&A integrations: Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks (PANW) appears primed to continue improving its margins despite dealing with the acquisition integrations of CyberArk and Chronosphere, according to Morgan Stanley.

"Management was clear that recent gross margin variability is primarily a function of mix rather than execution issues or anything structural," said Morgan Stanley analysts, led by Meta Marshall, in a Tuesday investor note. "Product gross margins continue to be impacted by a higher mix of lower-margin hardware appliances as well as component cost impacts."

"On the services side, gross margins in the latest quarter were slightly lower QoQ due to increasing mix of high-growth SaaS offerings, which should improve going forward with scaling efficiencies," she added. "Importantly, management reinforced its long-standing focus on profitability, particularly in the context of M&A. The company remains disciplined in ensuring acquisitions scale to Palo Alto's margin profile over time, which underpins confidence in the path to 40%+ FCF margins longer term."

Morgan Stanley rates the stock at Overweight with a $223 price target.

The investment firm also noted Palo Alto's traction across AI security, SIEM, SASE, and cloud security, as well as continued progress with its platformization strategy.

"Palo Alto Networks sees a long runway for platformizations, which remain a core growth driver given the 'single pane of glass' value proposition and resonates well with customers looking to consolidate an increasingly fragmented security stack," Marshall said. 

"Importantly, Palo Alto Networks is seeing improved sales execution across the portfolio, with more reps ramped to sell multi-product solutions (aided in part by AI), supporting broader platform adoption over time," she added.

Last week, Benchmark initiated coverage on Palo Alto with a Buy rating, citing evidence that its Next Gen Security annual recurring revenue is expanding at a "tremendous scale."

data.title: Ethereum stablecoin supply hits $180B ATH: Is ETH demand mispriced?
data.description:
What if Ethereum (ETH-USD)'s real growth story doesn't lie in price but in the money quietly flowing through it?

According to Token Terminal, stablecoin supply on Ethereum (ETH-USD) has hit an ATH of ~$180B, up ~150% in 3 years. With a 60% market share, the asset isn’t just leading but rather becoming the financial highway for digital dollars.

The bigger picture? Reportedly, up to ~$1.7T could move on-chain in four years. Even if ETH's share drops to 50%, that still implies ~$850B in potential inflows. Stablecoins tell the clearest story here. 

Tether (USDT-USD) leads with ~$94B, while USD Coin (USDC) follows at ~$55B. But what’s interesting is the rise of newer players—USDS, USDe, and PYUSD—slowly gaining ground and making the stablecoin space more competitive.

The bullish ETH momentum doesn't end here. Data from Artemis shows the asset added ~$9.4B in stablecoins in just one month, while Solana (SOL-USD) lost around ~$820M, hinting that the big-whale money is clearly moving toward the biggest altcoin right now.

Institutional flows are also aligning with this trend. Data from SoSoValue shows Bitcoin (BTC-USD) ETFs pulled ~$471M in inflows on April 6, led by BlackRock's (IBIT), while Ethereum (ETH-USD) ETFs recorded ~$120M in inflows, led by BlackRock’s (ETHA) at ~$60.82M, with zero outflows across top funds.

However, investors should note that in the short term, things are a bit shaky. The ETH price is hovering around ~$2.06K, down ~4% in a day, as global tensions pulled down risk assets like BTC and even dragged down some major crypto-linked stocks such as MicroStrategy (MSTR), Robinhood (HOOD), and Mara Holdings (MARA) to about 2%-6%.

But zoom out a bit, and the picture looks more promising with stablecoin flows steadily rising on Ethereum (ETH-USD). Analyst Ali Martinez recently shared that ~$1.8K is the key level to watch—if ETH holds above this, it could possibly open the path toward ~$4.9K.

data.title: Another deadline, another pause? Trump’s Iran clock may tick longer
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump's deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz could change if talks with the Middle Eastern country progress on Tuesday. 

Multiple reports said the U.S. is preparing to carry out "heavy strikes" against the country if the deadline is not met, but if talks progress, that can change. 

However, if the deadline ends without any agreement, then the U.S. is looking to launch an attack. Iran has also reportedly ended diplomatic talks with Washington. 

The news comes hours after Trump issued a warning where he threatened the destruction of “a whole civilization” if Tehran does not agree to U.S. terms to end the war in the area.

The president also gave a deadline of 8 p.m. ET for Iran to accept U.S. conditions and open the Strait of Hormuz, adding that all of Iran could be "taken out in one night" if the deadline isn't met.

On the other hand, reports surfaced that the U.S. has attacked military targets on Kharg Island.

Despite Iran allegedly cutting off communication with the U.S., Middle Eastern officials said talks with ceasefire mediators continue, although efforts are now complicated.

If Trump extended the deadline, this would be the fourth time to do so. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
